fact: December 4 is exactly 17 days before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - that's when days are getting shorter super fast, losing about 2 minutes of daylight every single day!
In Antarctica, December 4 falls during their endless summer days when the sun never sets - it just circles around the sky like a lazy merry-go-round!
Reindeer in the Arctic are typically growing their winter coats so thick by this date that they look like walking fuzzy pillows - their fur can be 2 inches deep!
December 4 is usually peak sap season for evergreen trees as they pump extra sticky, fragrant chemicals to protect themselves from freezing - which is why Christmas trees smell so amazing right now!
In Japan, this is often prime time for snow monkeys to start their famous hot spring baths - they've figured out that soaking in warm water feels incredible when it's freezing outside!
December 4 falls during Sagittarius season, which means if the zodiac were a school year, we'd be in the 'adventure and exploration' unit right before winter break.
Polar bears in the Arctic are typically at their hungriest right now because the sea ice is finally thick enough for them to hunt seals after months of waiting around.
In northern countries, sunset happens so early that by December 4, some places see the sun disappear before most kids even get home from school - like nature's hitting the lights-out button at 3 PM!
Owls are usually getting extra chatty around this date because it's their peak mating season - so those spooky hoots echoing through the night are actually love songs!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, December 4 typically means spring flowers are blooming everywhere and baby animals are being born - it's like nature's biggest birthday party!

The moon often looks extra huge and orange when it rises on December nights because it's traveling through the thickest part of Earth's atmosphere - like looking at it through a giant magnifying glass made of air!
